evals.prompt: I'm looking at our Python order processing module at src/services/order_service.py and it's gotten out of hand. The process_order function is like 200 lines long, there's duplicated validation logic between create_order and update_order, and several places are doing raw dict access like order['status'] instead of using typed objects. Can you audit this and suggest what to refactor?
evals.expected_output: Audit with caller counts, identify long method + duplicated code + type safety smells, present tradeoffs, propose specific refactoring steps

assertions.name: caller-count-audit
assertions.description: Runs caller count audit before proposing changes
assertions.check: Output mentions counting callers, call sites, or 'call count' for functions in order_service.py

assertions.name: identifies-long-method
assertions.description: Identifies process_order as a Long Method (>50 lines, multiple responsibilities)
assertions.check: Output identifies process_order as a long method, mentions >50 or >200 lines, or cites the Long Method code smell

assertions.name: identifies-duplicated-code
assertions.description: Identifies duplicated validation logic between create_order and update_order
assertions.check: Output mentions duplicated validation logic shared between create_order and update_order

assertions.name: identifies-type-safety-issue
assertions.description: Identifies raw dict access as a type safety boundary problem
assertions.check: Output mentions raw dict access, typed objects/dicts, type safety boundary, TypedDict, or 'parse_entry' pattern

assertions.name: before-after-diff
assertions.description: Includes at least one BEFORE/AFTER code diff
assertions.check: Output includes a code block showing BEFORE/AFTER comparison or diff-style lines with +/- markers

assertions.name: states-tradeoffs
assertions.description: Explicitly states what got better AND what got worse for each proposed change
assertions.check: Output explicitly mentions tradeoffs, costs, downsides, or 'what gets worse' for at least one proposed change

assertions.name: surgical-commit-plan
assertions.description: Proposes a sequence of small, separate commits
assertions.check: Output proposes at least 2 separate commits or mentions surgical/small/separate commits for the refactoring

evals.prompt: Our TypeScript React app has a data transformation module (src/transforms/entry-converter.ts) that's become a mess. There's a bunch of single-use helper functions that only get called once, a giant switch statement where 3 of the 5 branches do basically the same thing (serialize to string then wrap), and the types are defined by duplicating fields instead of using intersections. How should I clean this up?
evals.expected_output: Identify single-caller inlining candidates, collapse duplicate switch branches, derive types via intersection, surgical commit plan

assertions.name: inline-single-callers
assertions.description: Identifies single-use helper functions as inline candidates
assertions.check: Output identifies single-use or single-caller functions and recommends inlining them

assertions.name: collapse-duplicate-branches
assertions.description: Identifies 3-of-5 switch branches doing the same thing and proposes collapsing
assertions.check: Output identifies that multiple switch branches share the same pattern (serialize-then-wrap) and suggests collapsing them

assertions.name: type-intersection
assertions.description: Suggests using type intersections/composition instead of duplicating fields
assertions.check: Output mentions type intersection, type composition, '&' operator, or deriving types instead of duplicating shared fields

assertions.name: before-after-diff
assertions.description: Includes at least one BEFORE/AFTER code diff
assertions.check: Output includes a code block showing BEFORE/AFTER comparison or diff-style lines with +/- markers

assertions.name: states-tradeoffs
assertions.description: Explicitly states tradeoffs for proposed changes
assertions.check: Output explicitly mentions tradeoffs, costs, downsides, or 'what gets worse' for at least one proposed change

assertions.name: surgical-commit-plan
assertions.description: Proposes a sequence of small, separate commits
assertions.check: Output proposes at least 2 separate commits or mentions surgical/small/separate commits for the refactoring

evals.prompt: I'm reviewing a Java service — com.acme.payment.PaymentProcessor — that's become difficult to maintain. It has null checks nested 4 levels deep, validates shipping vs payment vs notification all in one 100-line method, and three different shipping strategies implemented as a long if-else chain. What's the right refactoring approach here?
evals.expected_output: Convert nested conditionals to guard clauses, extract Chain of Responsibility for validation, apply Strategy pattern for shipping, split Large Class by responsibility

assertions.name: guard-clauses
assertions.description: Proposes converting nested null checks to guard clauses / early returns
assertions.check: Output mentions guard clauses, early returns, or flattening/inverting nested conditionals for the null checks

assertions.name: chain-of-responsibility
assertions.description: Suggests Chain of Responsibility for the multi-step validation logic
assertions.check: Output mentions Chain of Responsibility pattern or chained validators for the validation method

assertions.name: strategy-pattern
assertions.description: Suggests Strategy pattern to replace the if-else shipping chain
assertions.check: Output mentions Strategy pattern or strategy interface/class for the shipping methods

assertions.name: large-class-split
assertions.description: Identifies Large Class smell and proposes splitting by responsibility
assertions.check: Output identifies Large Class smell or suggests splitting PaymentProcessor by responsibility (shipping, payment, notification)

assertions.name: five-axis-scan
assertions.description: Includes a five-axis pre-refactoring assessment scan
assertions.check: Output includes a pre-refactoring assessment covering at least 3 of: correctness, readability, architecture, security, performance

assertions.name: severity-labeling
assertions.description: Uses severity labels (Critical/Nit/Optional) for findings
assertions.check: Output labels at least one finding with a severity prefix like Critical, Nit, or Optional

evals.prompt: I refactored our order service and now the unit tests are failing — they were testing internal helper functions that I inlined. Should I update the tests to match the new structure? Also our test file is 800 lines with a lot of duplicated setup code, can we refactor that too while we're at it?
evals.expected_output: Warn that refactoring tests alongside production code breaks the safety net. Tests failing means either behavior changed (not refactoring) or tests were testing implementation details. Advise: revert if behavior changed, or separate test cleanup into its own dedicated pass. Do NOT refactor test file in same pass.

assertions.name: warns-against-test-refactor
assertions.description: Explicitly warns NOT to refactor test files alongside production code
assertions.check: Output explicitly says not to refactor tests together with production code, or warns that doing so destroys the safety net

assertions.name: diagnoses-test-failure
assertions.description: Diagnoses why tests broke: behavior change vs implementation-detail testing
assertions.check: Output analyzes root cause: whether tests broke from actual behavior change or from testing implementation details

assertions.name: separate-pass-guidance
assertions.description: Advises test cleanup in a separate, dedicated pass if needed
assertions.check: Output recommends doing test cleanup in a dedicated, separate pass from production refactoring

assertions.name: behavior-preservation-check
assertions.description: Checks whether behavior was actually preserved during the refactor
assertions.check: Output asks or verifies whether the refactoring preserved original external behavior

assertions.name: test-as-spec
assertions.description: References tests as the specification of expected behavior
assertions.check: Output describes tests as the specification, contract, or definition of expected behavior

evals.prompt: There's this weird null check in our Python data pipeline at src/pipeline/transform.py line 142 — it guards against a condition that I don't think can ever happen. The code works fine without it when I tested locally. Should I just remove it to simplify the flow?
evals.expected_output: Apply Chesterton's Fence: check git blame for why the null check was added, verify it's truly unreachable before removing. Show the five-axis scan for this specific change. If it's genuinely dead code, flag it and ask before deleting.

assertions.name: chestertons-fence
assertions.description: Mentions Chesterton's Fence by name or explains the concept
assertions.check: Output mentions 'Chesterton's Fence' or explains: don't remove code without understanding why it was added

assertions.name: git-blame-check
assertions.description: Suggests checking git blame before removing the null check
assertions.check: Output recommends checking git blame, git log, or version history before removing the guard code

assertions.name: five-axis-scan
assertions.description: Applies five-axis pre-refactoring scan to this specific change
assertions.check: Output applies a pre-refactoring assessment or five-axis scan to evaluate this specific null check removal

assertions.name: ask-before-delete
assertions.description: Advises asking/confirming before deleting the guard code
assertions.check: Output advises explicitly asking or getting confirmation before deleting the null check

assertions.name: dead-code-hygiene
assertions.description: Addresses dead code hygiene: lists what would be removed and asks confirmation
assertions.check: Output describes a dead code identification process: list what to remove, then confirm before deleting

evals.prompt: Our codebase has a utils/helpers.ts file that's become a dumping ground — there's formatDate, formatCurrency, parseDate, parseCurrency, and about 20 other functions. Some are used everywhere, some are used once, and a few appear to be unused. Also some functions take boolean flags like formatDate(date, true, false) which is impossible to understand at call sites. How should I approach cleaning this up?
evals.expected_output: Run caller count audit on every export, apply simplification patterns (boolean flags → options object, generic names → descriptive), identify dead code for deletion, inline single-callers, scope the cleanup to avoid touching unrelated code. Apply Rule of 500 if needed.

assertions.name: caller-count-audit
assertions.description: Runs caller count audit on every export in the helpers file
assertions.check: Output mentions counting callers, call sites, or auditing usage for each exported function

assertions.name: boolean-flag-pattern
assertions.description: Identifies boolean parameter flags and suggests options objects or separate functions
assertions.check: Output identifies boolean flag parameters and suggests options objects, named parameters, or separate functions

assertions.name: rule-of-500
assertions.description: Mentions the Rule of 500 or suggests automation for large changes
assertions.check: Output mentions 'Rule of 500', >500 lines threshold, or suggests automation/codemods for the large cleanup

assertions.name: dead-code-identification
assertions.description: Identifies unused functions and proposes deletion
assertions.check: Output identifies unused or dead functions and proposes removing them after confirmation

assertions.name: scope-discipline
assertions.description: Advises scoping cleanup to avoid touching unrelated code
assertions.check: Output advises limiting change scope or avoiding drive-by refactors of unrelated code

assertions.name: simplification-pattern-table
assertions.description: References simplification patterns (structural/naming/redundancy)
assertions.check: Output categorizes problems using simplification patterns like boolean flags, naming issues, or redundancy patterns

evals.prompt: Our team has been shipping features fast for the past year and technical debt has piled up. I can see duplicated validation logic in at least 6 places, our test coverage is around 35%, the OrderService class is 800 lines with 25 methods, we're still on React 16, and deployment is fully manual with no rollback procedure. Can you help us assess the debt and create a prioritized remediation plan?
evals.expected_output: Systematic debt inventory across all 5 categories (Code, Architecture, Testing, Documentation, Infrastructure) with quantified impact, ROI-based prioritized roadmap (quick wins / medium-term / long-term), and prevention strategy with quality gates

assertions.name: five-category-inventory
assertions.description: Inventories debt across all 5 categories: Code, Architecture, Testing, Documentation, Infrastructure
assertions.check: Output covers at least 4 of the 5 debt categories: Code, Architecture, Testing, Documentation, Infrastructure

assertions.name: quantified-impact
assertions.description: Quantifies debt impact in hours, cost, or frequency — not just qualitative descriptions
assertions.check: Output includes numerical estimates for at least 2 debt items (hours, monthly cost, frequency, or similar quantified metrics)

assertions.name: roi-prioritized-roadmap
assertions.description: Creates a prioritized remediation roadmap based on ROI, not just severity
assertions.check: Output presents a prioritized roadmap or action plan with at least 2 tiers (e.g., quick wins vs medium-term) and mentions ROI, effort, or savings for items

assertions.name: prevention-strategy
assertions.description: Recommends prevention strategy with quality gates or debt budget
assertions.check: Output recommends quality gates, debt budget, CI checks, or similar prevention mechanisms to avoid future debt accumulation

assertions.name: references-existing-methodology
assertions.description: Cross-references existing refactoring methodology (code smells, caller audit, etc.)
assertions.check: Output references caller count audit, code smells, or other refactoring methodology from the skill for specific code-level items
